elements.sent-2411-001: As AI-generated images become widespread, reliable watermarking is essential for content verification, copyright enforcement, and combating disinformation.
elements.sent-2411-002: Existing techniques rely on heuristic approaches and lack formal guarantees of undetectability, making them vulnerable to steganographic attacks that can expose or erase the watermark.
elements.sent-2411-003: Additionally, these techniques often degrade output quality by introducing perceptible changes, which is not only undesirable but an important barrier to adoption in practice.
elements.sent-2411-004: In this work, we introduce \tool, the first provably undetectable watermarking scheme for diffusion models.
elements.sent-2411-005: Our approach leverages the Continuous Learning With Errors (CLWE) problem --- a cryptographically hard lattice problem --- to embed watermarks in the latent noise vectors used by diffusion models.
elements.sent-2411-006: By proving undetectability via reduction to a cryptographically hard problem we ensure not only that the watermark is imperceptible to human observers or adhoc heuristics, but to any efficient detector that does not have the secret key.
elements.sent-2411-007: Empirical evaluations on state-of-the-art diffusion models confirm that \tool achieves high recoverability, preserves image quality, and is robust to minor perturbations such JPEG compression and brightness adjustments.
elements.sent-2411-008: Uniquely, \tool cannot be detected nor removed by recent steganographic attacks.
elements.sent-2411-009: In an era where AI-generated images are becoming increasingly common, identifying whether an image originates from a model is critical for combating disinformation and settling copyright claims.
elements.sent-2411-010: Watermarking, wherein a signal is inserted into an image such that it can later be recovered, has emerged as an essential tool to determine the provenance of images.
elements.sent-2411-011: Current approaches to watermarking AI-generated images remain ad-hoc.
elements.sent-2411-012: For example, \treering~\cite{treering was conjectured to be undetectable and robust, but no proof as such was given.}
elements.sent-2411-013: While an important step forward, \treering watermarks can be both detected and removed by standard steganalysis~\cite{steg_attack , i.e.\ averaging the difference between watermarked and non-watermarked images.}
elements.sent-2411-014: This is more than a security concern; it shows that the watermark affects the output of the model such that the output images are both quantitatively and qualitatively different from the original.
elements.sent-2411-015: After spending considerable effort to train and optimise large diffusion models, providers and users are understandably reluctant to then degrade the quality of the images they generate in order to watermark them.
elements.sent-2411-016: Thus for both security and quality reasons, it is important that the watermark be provably undetectable, by which we mean that a watermarked image be indistinguishable from an unwatermarked image.
elements.sent-2411-017: A rigorous reduction to a cryptographically hard problem is needed, otherwise it may turn out that a watermark that was conjectured to be undetectable is in fact detectable by some other means, or may affect downstream applications.
elements.sent-2411-018: By proving undetectability, we ensure that the watermark does not compromise the quality of the generated images and remains stealthy to detectors.
elements.sent-2411-019: In this work we will show how cryptographic techniques can be used to inject a watermark into diffusion model generated images such the watermark is provably indistinguishable from the no watermark case.
elements.sent-2411-020: Previous work on watermarking AI-generated images has shown that by injecting signals into the noise inputs of diffusion models, it is possible to recover watermarks even after significant modifications to the image by inverting the model and analyzing the noise for the watermark signal~\cite{treering,gaussian_shading .}
elements.sent-2411-021: While these techniques demonstrated robustness to perturbations arising in benign applications, they were not provably undetectable and more recent work has shown that they can be detected and removed by steganalysis~\cite{steg_attack .}
elements.sent-2411-022: Our approach is to modify the noise input to a diffusion model using a distribution based on the Continuous Learning With Errors (CLWE) problem~\cite{clwe .}
elements.sent-2411-023: The intuitive idea is that this distribution produces samples that appear normal in all directions except for a secret direction in which it has a signature pattern that is different from normal.
elements.sent-2411-024: By carefully constructing the watermark, we can prove that any process that can reliably detect the watermark can be used to distinguish a CLWE distribution from a normal distribution.
elements.sent-2411-025: This problem is known to be cryptographically hard~\cite{clwe , and thus we can be confident that \tool is undetectable for any downstream process, not just those we have tested, ensuring image quality and undetectability.}
elements.sent-2411-026: Moreover, because \tool only modifies the input noise, it does not modify the model in any way and does not require retraining.
elements.sent-2411-027: Meanwhile, if the secret direction is known then the watermark can be efficiently recovered by inverting the model and checking for the signature.
elements.sent-2411-028: A secondary advantage to this approach is that it supports multiple keys, and several keys can be used in a single watermark.
elements.sent-2411-029: For example, a provider may have a key for all images generated by a model and a key for each user, allowing them to quickly check if an image came from the model before checking which user generated it.
elements.sent-2411-030: In an ideal world, the perfect scheme would be such that watermarked images can always be verified to contain the watermark (completeness) and unmarked images can always be verified to not contain the watermark (soundness), without degrading image quality, and despite perturbations to the image that do not degrade image quality.
elements.sent-2411-031: Unfortunately, we prove in Section~\ref{sec:security_proof that such perfect watermarking is impossible theoretically.}
elements.sent-2411-032: As such, we must choose which properties are most important for our application.
elements.sent-2411-033: In this work we aim first for undetectability that can be rigorously proven, and then secondarily for a watermark that is as robust as possible.
elements.sent-2411-034: While \tool is theoretically undetectable, the question becomes whether it is usable in practice.
elements.sent-2411-035: Image sampling and inversion of diffusion models adds considerable noise, and thus the watermark may not be recoverable.
elements.sent-2411-036: If so, can the watermark be recovered after applying common perturbations? To that end, we evaluate \tool on commonly used diffusion models and datasets to verify undetectability, recoverability, image quality, and robustness.
elements.sent-2411-037: We show that \tool is indeed undetectable by common means, produces images with the same quality as the original, and is recoverable with the secret key.
elements.sent-2411-038: We also show that it is robust to small perturbations, such as JPEG compression and brightness.
elements.sent-2411-039: Our contribution is a novel and practical watermarking scheme, \tool, for images generated by modern diffusion AI models.
elements.sent-2411-040: It is provably undetectable, in the sense that detectability is as hard a distinguishing the CLWE distribution from a Gaussian---a standard cryptographic hardness assumption in lattice-based cryptography.
elements.sent-2411-041: Diffusion models, such as Stable Diffusion~\cite{podell2023sdxl,rombach2022high , have quickly emerged as the go-to AI models for generating very high quality and customizable images.}
elements.sent-2411-042: They have been adopted across diverse fields such as digital art and content creation, e-commerce, and aiding research in natural sciences (see survey~\cite{yang2023diffusion ).}
elements.sent-2411-043: The process to generate an image from a diffusion model is called ``sampling'', in which the model is given a text embedding and a noise vector (in the latent space of the model, hence this is often called the ``latent vector'' or ``latent noise''), and the model iteratively removes the noise to produce an image described by the text embedding, as illustrated in Figure~\ref{fig:ldm .}
elements.sent-2411-044: We refer to~\cite{rombach2022high, podell2023sdxl for understanding the details of diffusion models, and describe only the parts that are necessary for our work.}
elements.sent-2411-045: The noise vector is drawn from a standard normal (Gaussian) distribution, that is $\vec{z \sim \gaussian$.}
elements.sent-2411-046: Malicious actors can (and do) misuse diffusion models to create realistic fake images, like deepfakes, which pose serious risks.
elements.sent-2411-047: They use these images to blackmail~\cite{henry2024sextortion , execute scams~\cite{scam , spread disinformation~\cite{disinformation , and manipulating individuals with potentially harmful financial, social, and political consequences.}}}
elements.sent-2411-048: Watermarking is a proposed measure to detect AI-generated content.
elements.sent-2411-049: Model developers, such as OpenAI and Google, have pledged to watermark the content they generate with active monitoring and government oversight~\cite{uswatermark,pledge,euwatermark .}
elements.sent-2411-050: Watermarking an image refers to implanting a signal within it such that the signal can later be recovered.
elements.sent-2411-051: If an image can be confirmed to contain the watermark signal, this offers proof of the provenance of the image.
elements.sent-2411-052: In a secret key scheme, only those with the key can verify an image's origin.
elements.sent-2411-053: Consider a company that hosts a closed-source model.
elements.sent-2411-054: If the company watermarks the images generated by the model using a secret key, they can later verify whether an image was generated by the model by checking for the watermark using the key.
elements.sent-2411-055: They can also give the key to another entity to do verification, for example a social media platform that wants to verify the authenticity of images uploaded by users.
elements.sent-2411-056: The company may also assign a key to each user in order to trace which user was responsible for generating a particular image.
elements.sent-2411-057: Watermarks may be applied to images after generation (``post-process'') or during model execution (``in-process'').
elements.sent-2411-058: Diffusion models offer another option: watermarking the noise vector $\vec{z}$ used to generate the image (``pre-process''), as introduced by Tree Rings~\cite{treering .}
elements.sent-2411-059: In this work we focus on secret-key pre-process watermarking.
elements.sent-2411-060: See Figure~\ref{fig:wm_use_cases for an illustration of the use cases.}
elements.sent-2411-061: Despite the potential benefits of watermarking AI generated images, adoption has been limited~\cite{wsj_wm .}
elements.sent-2411-062: One reason for this is image quality.
elements.sent-2411-063: Large models that generate high-quality images are expensive to train and serve, and both companies and users are sensitive to any degradation in image quality.
elements.sent-2411-064: Further, changes to image quality may also affect the performance of downstream tasks.
elements.sent-2411-065: For example, if the generated images are used to train another model or as inputs to a larger process, any changes in the images may affect the performance of the system as a whole.
elements.sent-2411-066: Existing watermarking techniques for diffusion models have a noticeable impact on the images generated.
elements.sent-2411-067: Post-processing techniques, e.g.\ embedding a signal into the output pixel data and even AI-based techniques, have visible quality losses~\cite{treering .}
elements.sent-2411-068: As we evaluate in this work, state-of-the-art techniques such as \treering and \gs, despite aiming for not being perceptible, produce images with a significant quality differences from their unwatermarked equivalents (see Section~\ref{sec:quality ).}
elements.sent-2411-069: This is because of ad-hoc design choices and the lack of provable guarantees.
elements.sent-2411-070: When evaluated over a large set of images, it can be seen that the watermarked images are biased (less diverse statistically) than the unwatermarked images.
elements.sent-2411-071: Further, recent attacks have shown that the watermarks can be detected, removed, and forged using standard steganographic techniques~\cite{steg_attack .}
elements.sent-2411-072: In this work, we aim to solve this problem by designing {\em provably undetectable watermarking.}
elements.sent-2411-073: By this we mean that distinguishing between watermarked and unwatermarked images must be computationally at least as hard as solving a cryptographic problem.
elements.sent-2411-074: This ensures that there is a negligible difference between watermarked and unwatermarked images for any downstream application.
elements.sent-2411-075: Even a determined adversary trying just to detect the presence of a watermark faces a challenge at least as hard as breaking state-of-the-art cryptography.
elements.sent-2411-076: This approach embodies a secure by construction principle, in contrast to existing techniques which empirically evaluate against specific attacks but cannot rule out the existence of other effective attacks.
elements.sent-2411-077: In order to construct a provable scheme, we first formally define the watermarking process.
elements.sent-2411-078: In order to be useful, we require that a watermarking scheme be at least complete and sound, for which we use Definitions~\ref{def:wm_complete and~\ref{def:wm_sound .}}
elements.sent-2411-079: Before we can define undetectability, we must define formally what we mean by ``distinguishing'' and how we measure success.
elements.sent-2411-081: Define the advantage of $D$ in distinguishing $\mathcal{O _1$ and $\mathcal{O _2$ as: \begin{equation}Adv_{D, O_1, O_2} = \left| \Pr\left[ D^{O_1}(1^\lambda) = 1 \right] - \Pr\left[ D^{O_2}(1^\lambda) = 1 \right] \right|\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-082: We want to ensure that our watermarking scheme is undetectable, that is, that the output of the watermarking process is indistinguishable from non-watermarked images generated by the model.
elements.sent-2411-083: We formally define this in Definition~\ref{def:wm_undetectable .}
elements.sent-2411-084: Let $\mathcal{W _{ \theta, k}$ be an oracle that on input $\pi \in \tokenspace$ returns $\text{Mark _\theta(\pi; k)$ for a fixed key $k \leftarrow \text{Setup _\theta(1^\lambda)$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-085: A watermarking scheme $(\text{Setup , \text{Mark}$, $\text{Extract )$ is undetectable if for all {\em probabilistic polynomial-time PPT distinguishers $D$, $Adv_{D, \mathcal{M _{\theta , \mathcal{W _{ \theta, k \le negl(\lambda)$.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-086: Our approach to watermarking images is based on the idea of modifying the latent noise vector of a diffusion model (i.e.\ pre-process watermarking).
elements.sent-2411-087: Recall that a diffusion model takes as input a latent vector that is expected to be from the standard gaussian distribution.
elements.sent-2411-088: In our approach, we instead use a latent vector that is drawn from the hCLWE distribution (which will be defined in Section~\ref{sec:clwe ).}
elements.sent-2411-089: This vector is indistinguishable from the standard gaussian distribution to all PPT-computable functions without the secret key, but can be efficiently distinguished when the secret key is known.
elements.sent-2411-090: We call using hCLWE samples to generate an image the {\em watermark injection process.}
elements.sent-2411-091: To test whether an image is watermarked, a system with access to the secret key uses the {\em watermark recovery process.}
elements.sent-2411-092: During recovery, we invert the diffusion model to get an estimate of the initial latent vector from the watermarked image.
elements.sent-2411-093: We then use the secret key to determine if the latent vector is from the hCLWE distribution, and if so, then it is very likely that the image is watermarked using that key.
elements.sent-2411-094: In this section we describe the high-level approach of watermarking, including the necessary background on CLWE, and the watermark injection and recovery processes.
elements.sent-2411-095: We will prove undetectability of the construction in Section~\ref{sec:security_proof .}
elements.sent-2411-096: Consider the scatter plot shown in Figure~\ref{fig:clwe_scatter .}
elements.sent-2411-097: If you were to look at the distribution of the points along either axis, it would look like a standard gaussian distribution.
elements.sent-2411-098: But if you were to plot the points along the line $y = -x$, you would notice that the points are periodic.
elements.sent-2411-099: That is, that they're bunched together around $-1, -1/2, 0, 1/2, 1,$ and so on.
elements.sent-2411-100: In two dimensions you can see this immediately by looking at the scatter plot, but what if the points are in three dimensions? The points would form a sphere from nearly any angle you look at, except if you found the ``secret direction'' you would notice the the points form slices and are again periodic\footnote{The original CLWE presentation at \url{https://youtu.be/9CB4KcoRB9U?t=60 has an animation that illustrates this effect clearly.. .}}
elements.sent-2411-101: This is the essence of the homogenous Continuous Learning With Errors (hCLWE) problem\footnote{Homogenous CLWE is a special case of the more general CLWE problem.. .}
elements.sent-2411-102: Intuitively, it becomes harder to find the secret direction as you increase the number of dimensions, as the number of directions you have to check grows exponentially.
elements.sent-2411-103: Deciding whether a set of points (from now on let us call them samples) are normally distributed in all directions, or if there is a secret direction in which they are periodic has been proven to be as hard as solving worst-case lattice problems~\cite{clwe .}

elements.sent-2411-109: The hCLWE distribution assumes a number of high-dimensional samples, while the noise vector required for the initial diffusion model latents is a single vector.
elements.sent-2411-110: The solution is to divide the latent vector into a number of non-overlapping blocks, each of which is treated as a sample from the hCLWE distribution.
elements.sent-2411-111: The blocks must each be large enough to ensure undetectability, but small enough to ensure enough samples to recover the watermark even if some blocks are lost or corrupted by noise.
elements.sent-2411-112: As we will see in Section~\ref{sec:practical-parameters , for a latent vector of dimensions $4 \times 64 \times 64$ a block size of either $4 \times 1 \times 1$ or $4 \times 4 \times 4$ leads to a secure watermark, but for the moment we leave this as an implementation parameter.}
elements.sent-2411-113: In order to improve robustness to perturbations, we use a standard technique in image watermarking (see~\cite{al2007combined ): we apply the hCLWE samples in the frequency domain of the latents.}

elements.sent-2411-117: We now have an approach that allows us to embed an hCLWE distribution in the latent space of a diffusion model, which we then use the produce an image.
elements.sent-2411-118: The question becomes: how do we determine if an image contains a watermark? From recent work~\cite{hong2024exact,song2020denoising , we can invert the diffusion model to get an estimate of the original latent vector (assuming the image is not perturbed).}
elements.sent-2411-119: Dividing the latent vector into blocks is then trivial, but the resulting blocks are subject to noise and perturbation.
elements.sent-2411-120: Given these noisy blocks and the secret direction of the hCLWE distribution, how do we determine if the watermark is present?.
elements.sent-2411-121: First, we can measure the error from the ideal hCLWE distribution by computing the inner product with the secret direction to get a set of $z_i$ values: \begin{equation}z_i = \gamma \langle \vec{y}_i, \vec{w} \rangle \mod 1\end{equation} In the no-watermark case, we expect that for well-chosen parameters the $z_i$ values will be uniformly distributed in $[0, 1)$.
elements.sent-2411-122: In the watermark case, we expect that the $z_i$ values will be concentrated near zero, with the strength of the concentration depending on $\beta$, noise, and perturbations.
elements.sent-2411-123: This produces the situation shown in Figure~\ref{fig:clwe_error_hist , which shows the result of a statistical simulation comparing $z_i$ values calculated from samples drawn from a standard normal, hCLWE, and hCLWE with noise from a normal distribution of width $0.2$.}

elements.sent-2411-124: Thus our problem boils down to distinguishing two distributions.
elements.sent-2411-125: This is a well-studied problem; the Kolmogorov-Smirnov (KS) statistic is used for exactly this purpose.
elements.sent-2411-126: Intuitively, the KS statistic gives a measure of the difference between a set of samples from a reference distribution, with a higher score indicating that the samples are less likely to have been drawn that distribution.
elements.sent-2411-127: In our case we use the uniform distribution as the null hypothesis, and use KS to measure the distance of the given $z_i$ values from the uniform distribution.
elements.sent-2411-128: If the KS statistic is above a certain threshold, then we can reject the null hypothesis and say that the samples are not gaussian in the secret direction, and therefore the watermark is present.
elements.sent-2411-129: In concrete terms, the KS statistic is the maximum distance between the empirical CDF of the samples and the expected CDF of the reference distribution.
elements.sent-2411-130: As illustrated in Figure~\ref{fig:clwe_cdf , in the statistical simulation, the KS statistic for the standard normal samples is $0.036$, indicating a very small deviation from uniform, while the KS statistic for the hCLWE samples with $\beta = 0.1$ is $0.774$ indicating a large deviation from uniform.}
elements.sent-2411-131: Finally, the hCLWE samples with gaussian noise of variance $0.2$ produces a KS statistic of $0.575$, indicating a strong presence of the watermark despite the noise.

elements.sent-2411-141: Before we dive into the technical details of the proof, we first provide an intuitive explanation.
elements.sent-2411-142: Our proof is by reduction, that is if there exists a distinguisher that can reliably distinguish watermarked and unwatermarked images, then we can use that distinguisher to solve the decision hCLWE problem.
elements.sent-2411-143: Recall that in the hCLWE problem the distinguisher is given an oracle that for all queries returns samples from either the hCLWE distribution or the standard normal distribution.
elements.sent-2411-144: In the reduction, whenever the watermark distinguisher requests an image, we construct the latent vector from samples from the given oracle, which we give to the latent diffusion model to generate an image, before passing the image back to the watermark distinguisher.
elements.sent-2411-145: If the oracle gives us standard normal samples, then this corresponds to the unwatermarked case, while if the oracle gives us hCLWE samples, then this corresponds to the watermarked case.
elements.sent-2411-146: Other than the samples used to build the initial latent vector, the process is identical and thus the difference in probability between the two cases can only come from the samples.
elements.sent-2411-147: Note that if the latent vector has dimension $mn$, then each image generated requires $m$ samples from the oracle.
elements.sent-2411-148: If the watermark distinguisher has a non-negligible advantage, then so does our hCLWE distinguisher.

elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{Method to convert samples from a base distribution to hCLWE.} \label{alg:hclwe_sample} \KwIn{Secret direction $\vec{w}$, Samples $\vec{y}_1, \ldots, \vec{y}_m \in \reals^{n}$} \KwOut{Samples $\vec{y}'_1, \ldots, \vec{y}'_m \in \reals^{n}$} $\gamma' \gets \sqrt{\beta^2 + \gamma^2}$\; \For{$i \in [m]$}{ $k_i \gets \lceil \gamma' \langle \vec{y}_i, \vec{w} \rangle \rfloor$\; $z_i \gets \mathcal{N}(0, \beta)$\; $\vec{y}_i' \gets \vec{y}_i + \left( (z_i + k_i \cdot \gamma / \gamma') / \gamma' - \langle \vec{y}_i, \vec{w} \rangle \right) \vec{w}$\; } \KwRet{$\vec{y}'_1, \ldots, \vec{y}'_m$}

elements.sent-2411-174: We can now rewrite the output of the algorithm as: \begin{equation}\vec{y}' = \vec{y}^\perp + \left(\frac{z + k \cdot \gamma / \gamma'}{\gamma'}\right) \vec{w} = \vec{y}^\perp + \tilde{y}' \vec{w}\end{equation} From this we can write the PDF of $\vec{y '$ as the convolution of the choices of $k$ and $z$: \begin{equation}\rho(\vec{y}^\perp) \cdot \sum_{z \in Z} \rho_{\gamma'}(k) \rho_{\beta}(z)\end{equation} By substituting out $z$, we get: \begin{equation}\rho(\vec{y}^\perp) \cdot \sum_{z \in Z} \rho_{\gamma'}(k) \rho_{\beta / \gamma'}\left(\tilde{y}' - k \gamma / {\gamma'}^2 \right)\end{equation} This expression can be manipulated algebraically to achieve the same expression as in Definition~\ref{def:hclwe_dist .}}

elements.sent-2411-245: Watermarking images has been extensively studied since the 1990s~\cite{van1994digital .}
elements.sent-2411-246: Early research primarily focused on watermarking individual images, while more recent advancements target AI-generated images.
elements.sent-2411-247: Unlike traditional images, AI-generated content allows watermarking at various stages of the generation pipeline, including altering training data or fine-tuning models to embed watermarks.
elements.sent-2411-248: Post-processing techniques are commonly applied to individual images, embedding watermarks by modifying images after creation.
elements.sent-2411-249: Classical methods operate in either the spatial~\cite{van1994digital, tsai2000joint or frequency domain~\cite{al2007combined, guo2002digital, hamidi2018hybrid, kundur1997robust, lee2007reversible .}}
elements.sent-2411-250: Spatial domain techniques modify pixel intensities, often by altering the least significant bits of selected pixels.
elements.sent-2411-251: While efficient, these techniques lack robustness against simple transformations like brightness adjustments or JPEG compression.
elements.sent-2411-252: In contrast, frequency domain methods embed watermarks by altering an image's frequency coefficients after applying a transformation such as the Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT) or Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT).
elements.sent-2411-253: These approaches offer greater robustness to perturbations, while ensuring that the watermark remains imperceptible, often measured by peak signal-to-noise ratio (PSNR).
elements.sent-2411-254: More recently, many deep learning-based techniques have been introduced in order to enhance robustness and quality~\cite{jia2021mbrs, tancik2020stegastamp, zhu2018hidden, liu2019novel, ahmadi2020redmark .}
elements.sent-2411-255: These techniques typically leverage encoder-decoder neural architectures, where the encoder embeds a message into an image, and the decoder recovers the message.
elements.sent-2411-256: The models are trained to minimize differences between the watermarked and original images for even an adversarial classifier, while ensuring message recovery even in the presence of noise.
elements.sent-2411-257: Various architectures, noise injection strategies, and training objectives have been explored to achieve robustness against diverse transformations.
elements.sent-2411-258: However, existing post-processing methods lack provable guarantees of undetectability and may degrade image quality~\cite{treering, fernandez2023stable .}
elements.sent-2411-259: Additionally, using the same watermark across multiple images can expose vulnerabilities to steganographic attacks, allowing the watermark to be extracted~\cite{steg_attack .}
elements.sent-2411-260: Generative models like Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) and Latent Diffusion Models have become standard for producing high-fidelity, ultra-realistic images.
elements.sent-2411-261: When traditional watermarking methods are applied to AI-generated images, they often reduce visual quality~\cite{fernandez2023stable , which is clearly undesirable.}
elements.sent-2411-262: Recent research addresses this issue by embedding watermarks directly within the generative process~\cite{yu2020responsible, yu2021artificial, fernandez2023stable, treering, gaussian_shading, lukas2023ptw .}
elements.sent-2411-263: One approach involves training parts of the model to embed messages during generation, allowing extraction via a pre-trained decoder~\cite{yu2020responsible, fernandez2023stable, yu2021artificial, lukas2023ptw .}
elements.sent-2411-264: However, these techniques require multiple training rounds, which is computationally expensive, especially as the size of state-of-the-art models continues to grow.
elements.sent-2411-265: While these methods claim improved image quality over post-processing techniques, they rely on ad-hoc metrics like PSNR and are significantly more resource-intensive.
elements.sent-2411-266: Recent advances in watermarking latent diffusion model images propose modifying only the input latent vectors, offering computational efficiency without altering model parameters~\cite{treering, gaussian_shading .}
elements.sent-2411-267: Diffusion models then generate images normally, unaware of the modification to the latents.
elements.sent-2411-268: The watermarks are detected by reversing the diffusion process and examining latent vector.
elements.sent-2411-269: Techniques such as Tree Ring~\cite{treering and Gaussian Shading~\cite{gaussian_shading claim state-of-the-art performance in both visual quality, in the sense that they are imperceptible to human observers, and robustness, in the sense that after common image perturbations the watermark can still be recovered.}}
elements.sent-2411-270: However, none of these technique provide provable undetectability; the watermarks can still be identified, removed, or forged by steganographic attacks~\cite{steg_attack .}
elements.sent-2411-271: Our work advances these approaches by introducing a watermarking scheme that is provably undetectable and unforgeable.
elements.sent-2411-272: Our tool, \tool, achieves optimal image quality in latent diffusion model outputs yet watermarks cannot be identified or forged without the secret key.
elements.sent-2411-273: Uniquely, it leverages cryptographically hard lattice problems, specifically the CLWE (Continuous Learning with Errors) problem, to embed watermarks.
elements.sent-2411-274: This is a novel application in the context of generative models, as prior research using such cryptographic techniques has focused on implanting undetectable backdoors in random Fourier feature-based models for classification tasks~\cite{goldwasser2022planting .}
elements.sent-2411-275: In this work we introduced \tool, the first provably undetectable watermarking scheme for diffusion model generated images, leveraging the cryptographic hardness of the Continuous Learning With Errors (CLWE) problem.
elements.sent-2411-276: We first defined formally what it means for a watermark scheme to be undetectable, and then described how \tool works.
elements.sent-2411-277: We then proved that \tool is undetectable under the hCLWE assumption.
elements.sent-2411-278: Finally, we evaluated empirically the performance of \tool on common diffusion models and datasets, showing that it is usable in practice, produces images with much higher fidelity to the original unwatermarked images than existing techniques, and cannot be detected by steganographic techniques.
elements.sent-2411-279: Future work may consider improving the robustness of \tool to perturbations.
elements.sent-2411-280: Also, the concrete parameters for CLWE are not yet well studied, and it is possible that better parameters could improve the performance of \tool while maintaining undetectability.
elements.sent-2411-281: Finally, the technique used for inverting the model to obtain the estimate of the original latent vector is the current bottleneck in recovering the watermark in terms of both quality and speed.
elements.sent-2411-282: Improvements to the inversion method would significantly improve the performance of \tool.. \ifCLASSOPTIONcompsoc.

elements.sent-2411-471: In work concurrent to ours, \cite{prc_watermarks proposes using Pseudo-Random Codes (PRC) to bias the noise input to a diffusion model, similar to \gs but without requiring a nonce.}
elements.sent-2411-472: As the name suggests, PRCs are a new cryptographic primitive that can be considered a combination of encryption and error-correcting codes.
elements.sent-2411-473: Cyphertexts are generated by encoding a message with a secret key, which can then be decoded even if some subset of the bits are flipped.
elements.sent-2411-474: Without the secret key the cyphertext appears random to all computationally-bounded observers.
elements.sent-2411-475: Intuitively, the signs of the latent noise are chosen to match a PRC encoding of a 0-length message.
elements.sent-2411-476: By the pseudorandom nature of the PRC, this is equivalent to choosing the signs randomly, and therefore does not affect the model output.
elements.sent-2411-477: Similar to \treering and \gs, the model is inverted to obtain an estimate of the original noise vector, from which the sign values are extracted and checked using the PRC decoder.
elements.sent-2411-478: If the decoder outputs a valid message, the image is considered watermarked.
elements.sent-2411-479: In a sense, one could see \tool{ as a continuous-domain PRC using CLWE, in that with the key the presence of the code can still be detected despite noise, while without the key the samples are indistinguishable from a Gaussian.}
elements.sent-2411-480: Interestingly, in the appendix of~\cite{prc_watermarks , the authors describe that in order to achieve robustness to perturbations they had to use parameters that are not theoretically secure.}
elements.sent-2411-481: The parameters picked in \tool{ also do not match those of in prior theoretical reductions, but for a completely different reason: We are building on the security of CLWE against a sample-constrained adversary, which has not been theoretically studied thus far to our knowledge.}

elements.sent-2411-486: In this work we introduced \tool{ , a provably undetectable watermarking scheme for diffusion model generated images, leveraging the cryptographic hardness of the Continuous Learning With Errors (CLWE) problem.}
elements.sent-2411-487: We first defined formally what it means for a watermark scheme to be undetectable, and then described how \tool{ works.}
elements.sent-2411-488: We then proved that \tool{ is undetectable under the hCLWE assumption with limited samples.}
